fund.name: HSBC ELSS Tax Saver Fund - Direct Growth

allocations.rationale: Saves ₹30,000/year in tax under Section 80C (₹1.5L × 20% slab). Diversified equity fund with a 3-year lock-in — your money is working hard while you save tax. Equity-taxed, so long-term gains above ₹1.25L/year taxed at only 12.5%.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Ultra-low 0.20% yearly fee — you keep almost all your returns. Buys all 150 mid-sized Indian companies at market weight. No fund manager guessing, no overlap with ELSS. Midcaps grow faster over long periods (15-22 years).

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Automatically holds ~65% stocks + ~35% safer options. Gives you built-in stability without managing two separate funds. Equity-taxed (better than Conservative Hybrid which is slab-taxed). ₹12,500 Cr AUM = proven track record over 25 years.

fund.name: Nippon India ETF Gold BeES
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Gold goes up when stocks go down — it's your portfolio's shock absorber. 0.40% fee, ₹53,000 Cr AUM (India's largest gold ETF). Note: gold is slab-taxed at 20%, so it's not the most tax-efficient holding, but the diversification benefit outweighs this for a 14% allocation.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Kuvera, Groww, or MFU (all free, no commissions). Step 2: Set up 4 monthly SIPs — ₹12,500 (HSBC ELSS), ₹10,000 (Motilal Oswal Midcap 150 Index), ₹7,500 (ICICI Pru Equity & Debt), ₹5,000 (Nippon Gold BeES). Step 3: Invest your ₹8L existing corpus as lumpsum split across the same 4 funds (₹2.5L + ₹2.5L + ₹1.5L + ₹1.5L). Step 4: Set all SIP dates within 3 days of your salary credit. Step 5: Set up 10% annual SIP step-up — most apps do this automatically.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Verify all 4 SIPs are debiting correctly and your 80C deduction is being captured in payslip.
After 1 year: Check if any fund has consistently underperformed its category by more than 3% — if so, consider switching. Also review your SIP step-up was applied.
After 3 years: Your ELSS lock-in ends for the first year's investment. You can redeem if needed, but stay invested for long-term compounding.
After 5 years: Major check — is your home down payment fund on track? Adjust allocation if needed as this goal approaches.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year (say every January), check if your allocation has drifted more than 5% from target. If stocks have risen a lot and now occupy 91% instead of 86%, move some money from equity funds to the hybrid or gold fund to get back to target. Don't rebalance more than once a year — each switch can trigger taxes. At age 40+ (in ~9 years), gradually shift 5% from pure equity into the hybrid fund for more stability as retirement approaches.
plan.rationale: You're 31 with 22 years until retirement — that's enough time for stocks to compound significantly, but your 5-year home goal and moderate risk appetite mean we shouldn't go all-in on pure equity. The ELSS kills two birds: tax saving (₹30K/year at your 20% slab) and long-term growth. The Midcap 150 Index gives you growth power at near-zero fees (0.20% vs 1%+ for active midcap funds — over 22 years, that fee difference alone could mean ₹15-20L more in your pocket). The Aggressive Hybrid fund is your 'set and forget' stabiliser — it auto-balances between stocks and bonds. And 14% gold cushions you during market crashes. Four funds, four AMCs, zero complexity.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year. Your portfolio value will temporarily fall — this is normal and recovers over 3-5 year periods. Don't stop SIPs during dips; that's when you buy more units cheaply.

Your ₹60L home down payment goal in 5 years is ambitious. At ₹35K/month, you'll likely accumulate ₹35-40L in 5 years — you'll need your spouse to contribute similarly, or extend the timeline to 7-8 years.
Gold can stay flat for years and then spike. Don't expect smooth returns — it's insurance, not growth.
ELSS has a 3-year lock-in per installment. You can't withdraw each year's investment until 3 years later. Plan liquidity accordingly.
If you switch jobs or your tax bracket changes to the new regime, ELSS loses its 80C benefit — reassess at that point.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth. At 10% step-up, your ₹35K/month becomes ₹38.5K in year 2, ₹42.4K in year 3, and so on. This alone adds ₹1.5Cr+ to your retirement corpus over 22 years.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Age 41 — begin shifting 10% from pure equity to hybrid
Age 48 — 4 years to retirement, protect accumulated wealth

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: The equity allocation consists of HSBC ELSS (active, 36% of total portfolio, so 36/72 = 50% of equity) and Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund (passive, 29% of total, so 29/72 ≈ 40% of equity). Thus, ~40% of the equity sleeve is passive. However, the rationale praises low fees and 'no fund manager guessing' for the index fund, and the ELSS is not explicitly described as index-based, so it's assumed active. Therefore, score is around 0.45, slightly adjusted upward due to strong preference for passive traits.

turnover_score: Rebalancing is recommended only once per year, and only if allocations drift by more than 5%. No tactical shifts or frequent reviews are suggested. The plan explicitly warns against over-rebalancing due to tax implications. Review checkpoints are at 6 months, 1 year, 3 years, and 5 years — all long-term. Hence, turnover is low, scoring high on passivity (0.8).

cost_emphasis_score: The plan explicitly highlights low expense ratios (e.g., 0.20% for the index fund vs 1%+ for active funds) and quantifies the long-term savings (₹15-20L over 22 years). Cost is a central decision criterion, second only to tax efficiency. This strongly favours passive investing, warranting a 0.9.

research_vs_cost_score: There is no mention of company analysis, management quality, or stock-specific research. Fund selection is based on structure (index vs active), fees, tax treatment, and category diversification. The index fund is chosen specifically to avoid 'fund manager guessing'. This aligns with passive, research-light investing. Score: 0.85.

time_horizon_alignment_score: The plan is designed for a 22-year retirement horizon, with gradual glide paths starting at age 40+. SIP step-up is projected over decades. Even the 5-year home goal doesn't trigger short-term trading. The strategy emphasizes long-term compounding and discourages panic selling. Strongly long-term oriented: 0.9.

portfolio_activeness_score: Two equity funds: one passive (Motilal Oswal Midcap 150 Index), one likely active (HSBC ELSS). The Aggressive Hybrid fund is actively managed (65/35 split, no index mandate), and gold ETF is passive. Risk metrics are missing, but category and rationale suggest the equity portion is partially passive. The index fund has no tracking error or alpha mentioned, and low fee confirms passivity. Overall, the portfolio blends passive and active elements, but cost focus and index use tilt it toward moderate passivity. Given the significant active components (ELSS, hybrid fund), score is 0.6 — moderately passive but not fully.

pqs.reasoning:
The investment plan is highly aligned with Elizabeth Bennet's goals, life stage, and moderate risk appetite. She has a 22-year investment horizon with key goals including a 5-year home down payment, child education (15 years), and retirement (22 years). The plan balances growth and stability: 65% in diversified equity (ELSS + Midcap Index) for long-term compounding, 21% in Aggressive Hybrid for automatic equity-debt balance, and 14% in gold for diversification and downside protection. This allocation acknowledges her moderate risk appetite and the need for some stability as her 5-year goal approaches.

Diversification is strong: across asset classes (equity, hybrid, gold), market caps (midcap exposure via index fund), geographies (domestic focus is acceptable given investor preference and context), and fund houses (4 different AMCs). No thematic or sectoral concentration. Gold adds non-correlated asset exposure.

Risk-return is appropriate: projected returns of 11.5% base case are realistic for this allocation. Risks are clearly disclosed (market volatility, home goal shortfall, gold underperformance, ELSS lock-in). The plan includes SIP step-up (10% annually) and rebalancing guidelines, enhancing discipline. The shift toward conservative allocation over time (from 72% to 50% equity) matches her aging profile.

The plan is internally consistent: rationale, allocations, fund choices, rebalancing, and risk disclosures all support a coherent moderate-growth strategy. SIP and lumpsum amounts add up correctly to ₹35,000/month and ₹800,000 lumpsum. Review checkpoints align with key decision points (tax tracking, performance, lock-in expiry, goal proximity).

Tax efficiency is excellent: investor is in 20% tax slab (old regime), so ELSS is correctly used to claim full ₹1.5L 80C benefit, saving ₹30,000/year. The plan explicitly justifies ELSS on tax and growth grounds. Aggressive Hybrid (65%+ equity) is correctly chosen over Conservative Hybrid because it qualifies for equity taxation (LTCG 12.5% above ₹1.25L) vs slab taxation for debt-oriented hybrids. Gold is honestly flagged as slab-taxed but justified for diversification. Midcap index fund is efficient and low-cost. No recommendation of slab-taxed debt funds for core fixed-income need — instead uses equity-taxed hybrid. All tax-aware decisions are clearly explained.
